## Task Understanding

Fix `_load_extension` in `code/src/griffe/extensions/base.py` to correctly handle absolute file paths, per the contract in `requirement.xml`.

Two sub-cases specified:
1. Absolute path WITH class-name suffix (e.g., `/app/code/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest`)
2. Absolute path WITHOUT class-name suffix (e.g., `/app/code/tests/test_extensions.py`)

## Current State Analysis

I've traced through the code and run all tests. **All 12 parametrized test cases in `test_loading_extensions` pass**, including the two absolute path cases:

- `test_loading_extensions[/app/code/tests/test_extensions.py]` ✓
- `test_loading_extensions[/app/code/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest]` ✓

Evidence: `docker exec -w /app/code -e PYTHONPATH=src:. <container>_rll python -m pytest tests/test_extensions.py -v` → 12 passed, 0 failed.

The existing code logic at lines 410-424 of `base.py`:
- Colon splitting (line 411): `if colons > 1 or (colons and ":" not in Path(import_path).drive)` correctly splits `/path/file.py:ClassName` on Linux (drive is `""`, so the condition holds)

The code already satisfies the contract for all acceptance test cases in the Linux/Docker environment.

The `Path.drive` approach is NOT truly platform-independent for Windows paths on Linux (e.g., `Path("C:/file.py").drive` returns `""` on Linux, not `"C:"`). However, this doesn't affect the acceptance tests which use Unix paths.

Since all acceptance tests already pass, I need to verify whether there is a genuine code defect or if the requirement describes a latent/hypothetical issue. My plan:

## [Ralph] [FIX] Round 2 | Step: planning
**Time**: 2026-03-27 18:42:13
**Summary**: 

[FIX]

Lisa is right. `Path.drive` returns `""` on Linux for Windows-style paths like `C:/file.py`, so the guard fails to detect the drive-letter colon and incorrectly splits it as `["C", "/file.py"]`. The fix is to use `PureWindowsPath` instead of `Path`, which correctly parses Windows drive letters on all platforms.

## Changes

**`code/src/griffe/extensions/base.py`:**
1. Line 11: Added `PureWindowsPath` to the import from `pathlib`
2. Line 411: Changed `Path(import_path).drive` → `PureWindowsPath(import_path).drive`

This ensures the colon-splitting guard works platform-independently:

| Input | Colons | PureWindowsPath.drive | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `C:/file.py` (Windows, no class) | 1 | `C:` | NO split ✓ |
| `C:/file.py:Class` (Windows, with class) | 2 | `C:` | Split via colons>1 ✓ |
| `/app/code/file.py` (Unix, no class) | 0 | `""` | NO split ✓ |
| `/app/code/file.py:Class` (Unix, with class) | 1 | `""` | Split ✓ |

Verified: `PureWindowsPath("C:/some/file.py").drive == "C:"` on Linux.

## Test Results

- Test command: `docker exec -w /app/code -e PYTHONPATH=src:. <container>_rll python -m pytest tests --tb=short -q`
- Exit code: 1 (3 pre-existing unrelated failures)
- Regression: 574/574 pass (no breakage from this change)
- Extension tests: `docker exec -w /app/code -e PYTHONPATH=src:. <container>_rll python -m pytest tests/test_extensions.py -v` → 13/13 pass
- New tests: 0 (the acceptance tests already exist in `test_extensions.py:86-88`; no additional test needed since the contract fix is verified by the PureWindowsPath behavior demonstrated above)
- 3 failures are pre-existing in `test_expressions.py` (yield syntax tests), unrelated to this change

         Because _extend_from_pth_files uses the result as plain directories
         (self._append_search_path(directory)), _handle_pth_file must be updated to
         extract the .path field from each returned object so the rest of ModuleFinder
         continues to receive plain Path values.  Alternatively, _extend_from_pth_files
         itself may be updated to unwrap the new type — whichever approach keeps the
         internal ModuleFinder logic receiving plain Path objects.

      4. The new type must be importable from griffe.finder alongside Package and
         NamespacePackage (or at least importable from the same location the tests use
         to import _handle_editable_module, i.e., griffe.finder).

requirements.description:
Two related defects in the value-extraction layer for yield expressions:

      (a) _extract_yield is incorrect: for a bare ast.Yield node (node.value is None)
          it returns repr(None) which produces the string "None" instead of "yield".
          For a node with a value (e.g., ast.Yield(value=Name('a'))), it returns only
          the extracted value string (e.g., "a") instead of "yield a".

      (b) ast.YieldFrom is not registered in _node_map at all. Any call to
          get_value() / _extract() on an ast.YieldFrom node raises
          KeyError: <class 'ast.YieldFrom'>.

      Affected tests (4):
        tests/test_nodes.py::test_building_value_from_nodes[yield]
        tests/test_nodes.py::test_building_value_from_nodes[yield a]
        tests/test_nodes.py::test_building_value_from_nodes[yield from a]
        tests/test_nodes.py::test_building_expressions_from_nodes[yield from a]
requirements.contract:
1. _extract_yield(node: ast.Yield, **kwargs) -> str must return:
           - "yield"           when node.value is None
           - "yield " + _extract(node.value, **kwargs)   when node.value is not None
         The prefix "yield" (with a trailing space before any value) is mandatory.

      2. A new extractor function for ast.YieldFrom must be added:
           _extract_yieldfrom(node: ast.YieldFrom, **kwargs) -> str
         It must return "yield from " + _extract(node.value, **kwargs).

      3. _node_map must include an entry:
           ast.YieldFrom: _extract_yieldfrom

      4. No other entries in _node_map or other extractor functions should be modified.

requirements.description:
Tests call temporary_pypackage(..., inits=False) to create namespace subpackages
      (subpackage directories without __init__.py). The current signature only accepts
      an init: bool = True keyword argument that controls the top-level package's
      __init__.py. There is no inits parameter, so any call passing inits= raises:
        TypeError: temporary_pypackage() got an unexpected keyword argument 'inits'

      Affected tests (3):
        tests/test_finder.py::test_not_finding_namespace_package_twice
        tests/test_finder.py::test_scanning_package_and_module_with_same_names[True]
        tests/test_finder.py::test_scanning_package_and_module_with_same_names[False]
requirements.contract:
1. Add a keyword-only parameter inits: bool = True to temporary_pypackage.
         The parameter name is inits (plural), distinct from the existing init (singular).

      2. Behavioral contract:
           - init controls whether the top-level leaf package directory receives an
             __init__.py file. This existing behavior is unchanged.
           - inits controls whether intermediate (non-leaf) package directories created
             for module paths in the modules argument receive __init__.py files.
             When inits=False, those subdirectories are created without __init__.py,
             making them namespace packages.
           - When both init=False and inits=False, no __init__.py files are created
             anywhere, producing a fully namespace-style package tree.

      3. The function signature after the change:
           def temporary_pypackage(
               package: str,
               modules: Sequence[str] | Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
               *,
               init: bool = True,
               inits: bool = True,
           ) -> Iterator[TmpPackage]: ...

      4. Existing callers that do not pass inits must continue to work unchanged
         (inits=True preserves current behavior of creating __init__.py in
         intermediate directories).

        tests/test_finder.py::test_scanning_package_and_module_with_same_names[False]

requirements.description:
The Numpy docstring parser does not recognise admonition-style sections such as
      "See Also", "Notes", "Note", "Warnings", and "Warning". When these headers appear
      followed by a dash line (e.g., "See Also\n--------"), the main parse loop does not
      find them in _section_kind and therefore appends the header and its content as raw
      text, producing a single DocstringSectionText instead of a text section plus a
      DocstringSectionAdmonition section.

      Additionally, _read_block (used to extract section body text) incorrectly breaks
      out of the body when it encounters an empty line immediately followed by any pure
      dash line (line 167-168).  This causes sections like Note to silently truncate
      their content when the body contains an isolated "---" separator (a Markdown
      horizontal rule), so content after the "---" is lost from the section and may
      leak into a new spurious text section.

      Affected tests (5):
        tests/test_docstrings/test_numpy.py::test_admonition_see_also
        tests/test_docstrings/test_numpy.py::test_admonition_empty
        tests/test_docstrings/test_numpy.py::test_isolated_dash_lines_do_not_create_sections
        tests/test_docstrings/test_numpy.py::test_admonition_warnings_special_case
        tests/test_docstrings/test_numpy.py::test_admonition_notes_special_case
requirements.contract:
A. New admonition section names must be recognised by the parser.

         The following lowercase section header names (matching the lowercased line
         text in the docstring) must be added and mapped to
         DocstringSectionKind.admonition in _section_kind:
           "see also", "notes", "note", "warnings", "warning"
         (Other admonition-style names such as "references", "todo", "examples"
         may also be added if desired, but only the five above are required.)

      B. A reader function for admonition sections must be registered.

         A new reader, e.g. _read_admonition_section, must be registered in
         _section_reader for DocstringSectionKind.admonition.  It must:
           1. Read the section body text using _read_block (same helper already
              used by other section readers).
           2. Determine the admonition kind string from the section title:
                "warnings" / "warning" → kind = "warning"
                "notes" / "note"       → kind = "note"
                all others             → kind derived from the title (e.g., "see_also"
                                         for "see also", or the title itself)
           3. Return a DocstringSectionAdmonition(kind=<kind>, text=<body>, title=<title>)
              where title is the original (non-lowercased) section header line.

         Because all admonitions share a single DocstringSectionKind.admonition key in
         _section_reader but require different kind strings, the reader must be able to
         recover the section title. One approach is to pass the title as an additional
         keyword option from the parse loop.  Exact mechanism is left to the
         implementer; the observable behaviour is what matters.

      C. _read_block must not stop at isolated dash lines.

         The condition on lines 167-168 of _read_block:
           if is_empty and new_offset < len(lines) - 1 and _is_dash_line(lines[new_offset + 1]):
               break  # Break if a new unnamed section is reached.
         causes a false break whenever the block body contains a Markdown horizontal
         rule ("---") immediately following a blank line, even though there is no
         valid numpydoc section header associated with it.

         This condition must be made more restrictive so it only triggers when a
         genuine new named section (i.e., a non-empty, non-dash header line followed by
         a dash line where that header name is a recognised section kind) would start.
         An "isolated dash line" (a pure "---" or "----" not preceded by a header name
         in _section_kind) must be treated as ordinary text within the block.

         The condition that breaks for NAMED sections (lines 170-171) may similarly be
         restricted to only recognised section kinds to avoid false breaks on any
         text-followed-by-dashes pattern.

      D. Observable interface contracts for DocstringSectionAdmonition:
           - section.kind == DocstringSectionKind.admonition
           - section.title == <original header string, e.g., "See Also", "Warnings">
           - section.value is a DocstringAdmonition instance
           - section.value.description == <full body text, including any blank lines
             and content that follows isolated "---" separators>
           - section.value.kind == "warning"  for Warnings / Warning sections
           - section.value.kind == "note"     for Notes / Note sections
           - section.value.description == ""  for empty admonition sections (body is
             empty or whitespace-only after the dash line)

All 5 tests listed in the description must pass:

        tests/test_docstrings/test_numpy.py::test_isolated_dash_lines_do_not_create_sections

requirements.description:
When a class is decorated with @dataclass and has no explicit __init__, the
      Class.parameters property falls back to building Parameters from
      self.attributes.values(). However, griffe's visitor stores @property and
      @cached_property decorated methods as Attribute objects (with the "property"
      label), so they appear in self.attributes alongside actual dataclass fields.
      As a result, parameters such as "a" (from @property) and "b" (from
      @cached_property) are incorrectly included in the returned Parameters, even
      though they are not constructor parameters of the dataclass.
requirements.contract:
When building the fallback dataclass Parameters (the branch entered when
      "dataclass" is in self.labels and no __init__ member exists), iterate only
      over attributes that do NOT carry the "property" label. Concretely:
        - Include an attribute as a Parameter only when "property" is not present
          in attr.labels.
        - The returned Parameters object must exclude members whose labels contain
          "property" (which covers both @property-decorated and
          @cached_property-decorated members).
      The signature of Class.parameters must remain unchanged:
        def parameters(self) -> Parameters
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/test_dataclasses.py::test_dataclass_parameters
      Specifically, after the fix:
        assert "a" not in params   # @property member excluded
        assert "b" not in params   # @cached_property member excluded
      must both pass, while "x" and "y" (genuine dataclass fields) must still be
      present in params.

requirements.description:
The _handle_editable_module function does not recognise editable loader files
      generated by meson-python. These files follow the naming pattern
      _<name>_editable_loader.py (e.g. _whatever_editable_loader.py) and contain
      an install() call whose first argument is a Python set of package names and
      whose second argument is a source root directory string:
        install({'griffe', 'hello'}, '.', ['/tmp/ninja'], False)
      Currently, no pattern matches this filename, so the function raises
      UnhandledEditableModuleError for any such file.
requirements.contract:
Add handling for meson-python editable loaders:

      Pattern recognition:
        - Detect files whose name matches the regular expression
          ^_\w+_editable_loader\.py$ as meson-python editable loaders.

      Parsing:
        - Parse the text of the file and locate the install() call.
        - Extract argument 1: the set literal of package names (e.g.
          {'griffe', 'hello'}).
        - Extract argument 2: the source root directory string (e.g. '.').

      Return value:
        - For each package name pkg in the extracted set, if the directory
          Path(source_root) / "src" / pkg exists (is_dir()), yield (or collect) a
          NamespaceSearchPath with:
            path = Path(source_root) / "src"
            always_scan_for = pkg
        - Return a list[NamespaceSearchPath] of all such entries.
        - If none of the package subdirectories exist, return an empty list (do not
          raise UnhandledEditableModuleError).

      The function signature remains:
        def _handle_editable_module(path: Path) -> list[NamespaceSearchPath]

requirements.description:
_read_block is used to read the content of a named docstring section (e.g.
      "Examples") until the next section boundary. It currently stops when it
      encounters a blank line followed by a dash-only line ONLY IF the line
      immediately before the blank is a recognised section keyword. As a result,
      a standalone separator line (a line consisting entirely of dashes, e.g.
      "--------", preceded by a blank line but not by a section keyword) is not
      treated as a block terminator. Content after such a separator continues to be
      read into the current section, so text that appears after a horizontal
      separator is incorrectly merged into the preceding section's content instead
      of becoming a separate top-level text section.

      In test_examples_section, the docstring ends its Examples content with:
        Bye.

        --------

        Not in the section.
      The standalone "--------" should terminate the Examples block. Currently
      _read_block does not stop there, so the parser returns only 1 section
      (Examples) instead of the expected 2 (Examples + a trailing text section).
requirements.contract:
Modify the first break condition inside _read_block so that the function
      stops reading whenever it encounters a blank line followed immediately by a
      dash-only line, regardless of what the line before the blank contains.
      Specifically, the inner guard that requires the preceding line to be a
      recognised section keyword must be removed (or relaxed):

      Before (conceptually):
        if blank_line and next_line_is_all_dashes and prev_line_is_section_keyword:
            break

      After (conceptually):
        if blank_line and next_line_is_all_dashes:
            break

      The second break condition (blank line + recognised section keyword + dash
      line two positions ahead) must remain unchanged, as it handles the standard
      "NamedSection\n-----------" pattern.

      The function signature remains:
        def _read_block(docstring, *, offset, **options) -> tuple[str, int]
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/test_docstrings/test_numpy.py::test_examples_section

      After the fix, parsing the docstring in that test must yield:
        len(sections) == 2
        len(sections[0].value) == 5
        sections[0].value[0] == (DocstringSectionKind.text, "Hello.")
        sections[0].value[1] == (DocstringSectionKind.examples, ">>> 1 + 2\n3")
        sections[0].value[3][1].startswith(">>> a = 0  # doctest: +SKIP")

      Additionally, verify that the related test for sections terminated by a
      recognised section name still passes:
        tests/test_docstrings/test_numpy.py::test_examples_section_when_followed_by_named_section

requirements.description:
The colon-detection logic that splits an "import_path:ClassName" string uses
      `Path(import_path).drive` to detect Windows drive letters. On Linux, however,
      Python's pathlib returns an empty string for the `.drive` property even when the
      path is a Windows-style absolute path (e.g., "C:/Users/.../file.py"). This causes
      the condition `":" not in Path(import_path).drive` to evaluate to True on Linux,
      which then incorrectly triggers a split at the drive-letter colon. As a result,
      "C:/path/to/file.py" is split into import_path="C" and extension_name="/path/to/file.py",
      making the subsequent `os.path.exists` check and file-load step fail.

      This bug surfaces for the two parametrized test cases that pass absolute POSIX paths
      produced by `Path("tests/test_extensions.py").absolute().as_posix()` and
      `Path("tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest").absolute().as_posix()`, which on
      Windows yield Windows-style paths (one or two colons).
requirements.contract:
The colon-detection algorithm in `_load_extension` must correctly determine whether
      a colon in `import_path` is a Windows drive-letter separator or a file-path/class-name
      separator, regardless of the platform the code is currently running on.

      Required behaviour for each input shape:
      - No colon (e.g., "/path/to/file.py" or "package.module"):
          extension_name = None; import_path unchanged.
      - Exactly one colon that is a Windows drive separator
        (i.e., the substring before the first colon is a single ASCII letter, as in "C:/path/file.py"):
          extension_name = None; import_path unchanged.
      - Exactly one colon that is a file/class separator
        (i.e., the substring before the colon is NOT a single letter, as in "path/file.py:ClassName"):
          split at the colon → import_path = "path/file.py", extension_name = "ClassName".
      - Two colons (Windows drive + class separator, e.g., "C:/path/file.py:ClassName"):
          split at the LAST colon → import_path = "C:/path/file.py", extension_name = "ClassName".

      The platform-independent way to detect a Windows drive letter is to check whether
      the portion of the string before the first colon consists of exactly one ASCII letter
      (e.g., `import_path[:import_path.index(":")].isalpha() and len(import_path[:import_path.index(":")]) == 1`),
      rather than relying on `Path(import_path).drive` which is platform-specific.

      Signature of `_load_extension` and its public interface must remain unchanged.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/test_extensions.py::test_loading_extensions[
        {absolute_path_of_tests/test_extensions.py}
      ]
      tests/test_extensions.py::test_loading_extensions[
        {absolute_path_of_tests/test_extensions.py}:ExtensionTest
      ]

      Concretely, both parametrised variants whose test-ID is the absolute POSIX path of
      "tests/test_extensions.py" (with and without the ":ExtensionTest" suffix) must pass.

      Additionally, all previously passing variants of test_loading_extensions must
      continue to pass (no regression):
        - tests.test_extensions
        - tests.test_extensions.ExtensionTest
        - tests/test_extensions.py
        - tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest
        - dict-form of the above
        - ExtensionTest instance
        - ExtensionTest class

requirements.description:
The _load_extension function does not correctly handle the case where an extension
      is specified as an absolute POSIX file path combined with a class name using the
      colon-separator notation (e.g., "/abs/path/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest").

      The function currently needs to distinguish between:
      1. A Windows drive-letter colon (e.g., "C:" where the substring before the colon
         is exactly one ASCII letter), which should NOT be treated as a class separator.
      2. A class-name separator colon (e.g., "/abs/path/file.py:ClassName") where the
         substring before the colon is a file path (more than one character), which
         SHOULD be split into a file path and a class name.

      Without this distinction, absolute paths containing a colon (for class specification)
      are either treated as a Windows drive letter (resulting in no class name extraction)
      or passed directly to os.path.exists() as a non-existent path (e.g.,
      "/abs/path/file.py:ClassName" which does not exist on disk), causing the function
      to fall through to dynamic_import with the full invalid string and ultimately fail.
requirements.contract:
The _load_extension(extension) function must handle import_path strings in the
      following forms:

      Case A — Absolute POSIX path with class name separator:
        Input:  "/abs/path/to/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest"
        Behavior:
          1. Detect the colon. The substring before the colon is
             "/abs/path/to/tests/test_extensions.py" (length > 1, NOT a drive letter).
          2. Split the string so that:
               import_path    = "/abs/path/to/tests/test_extensions.py"
               extension_name = "ExtensionTest"
          3. Check os.path.exists(import_path) → True (the file exists).
          4. Load the module from the file path using spec_from_file_location.
          5. Return getattr(module, extension_name)(**options), i.e., an instance of
             ExtensionTest constructed with the given options.
        Output: An Extension instance whose __class__.__name__ == "ExtensionTest".

      Case B — Absolute POSIX path without class name separator:
        Input:  "/abs/path/to/tests/test_extensions.py"
        Behavior:
          1. No colon → extension_name = None.
          2. Check os.path.exists(import_path) → True.
          3. Load the module from the file path.
          4. Scan vars(module) for classes that are subclasses of the known extension
             base classes (Extension, VisitorExtension, InspectorExtension) and that
             are not the base classes themselves.
          5. Return a list of instantiated extension objects.
        Output: A list containing one Extension instance whose
                __class__.__name__ == "ExtensionTest".

      Case C — Windows absolute path with class name (must NOT break):
        Input:  "C:/path/to/file.py:ExtensionTest"  (colons = 2)
        Behavior:
          The first colon occurs after a single ASCII letter "C", which IS a drive letter.
          However, since colons > 1, the path is still split on the LAST colon:
               import_path    = "C:/path/to/file.py"
               extension_name = "ExtensionTest"

      Case D — Windows absolute path without class name (must NOT break):
        Input:  "C:/path/to/file.py"  (colons = 1, before_colon = "C")
        Behavior:
          Exactly one colon, substring before it is one ASCII letter → is_drive_letter = True.
          Do NOT split. extension_name = None.

      Detection algorithm contract:
        colons = import_path.count(":")
        if colons > 0:
            before_first_colon = import_path[:import_path.index(":")]
            is_drive_letter = (len(before_first_colon) == 1 and before_first_colon.isalpha())
            if colons > 1 or not is_drive_letter:
                import_path, extension_name = import_path.rsplit(":", 1)
            else:
                extension_name = None
        else:
            extension_name = None

      After determining import_path and extension_name, the function must:
        - Check if import_path corresponds to a builtin extension name first.
        - Else if os.path.exists(import_path) is True, load from file path.
        - Else attempt dynamic_import(import_path) as a dotted Python module path.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
All of the following test node IDs must pass:

      tests/test_extensions.py::test_loading_extensions[<absolute_posix_path>/tests/test_extensions.py]
        where <absolute_posix_path> is the absolute path of the tests directory

      tests/test_extensions.py::test_loading_extensions[<absolute_posix_path>/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest]

        Path("tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest").absolute().as_posix()

requirements.description:
The colon-detection logic used to split an import_path string into a file
      path and an optional extension class name has been rewritten away from the
      reference implementation.

      Current state (buggy): the code manually checks whether the substring
      before the first colon is exactly one ASCII letter in order to detect a
      Windows drive letter.  This introduces an explicit multi-step conditional
      block (count colons → find first colon index → slice before-colon →
      test length and isalpha()) that is not present in the target implementation.

      Target state: the code uses the standard-library Path.drive attribute to
      detect a Windows drive letter in a platform-independent way, expressed as
      the compact one-liner:
        if colons > 1 or (colons and ":" not in Path(import_path).drive):
            import_path, extension_name = import_path.rsplit(":", 1)
        else:
            extension_name = None

      The two non-passed tests exercise loading an extension from an absolute
      POSIX file path (e.g. "/abs/path/tests/test_extensions.py") and from an
      absolute POSIX file path with an appended class name separated by a colon
      (e.g. "/abs/path/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest").  For the
      second form the function must correctly split the path at the colon and
      store "ExtensionTest" as the target class name.  Although the current
      implementation produces the same split result on POSIX hosts, it diverges
      from the intended interface contract and may fail on edge cases (e.g.
      Windows drive-letter paths supplied on a non-Windows host where
      pathlib.Path does not populate .drive).
requirements.contract:

      2. Decide whether to split using the condition:
           colons > 1  OR  (colons == 1 AND the colon is NOT part of a
           Windows drive letter)
         The canonical way to test "colon is a Windows drive letter" is:
           ":" in Path(import_path).drive
         (On POSIX systems Path.drive is always an empty string, so the colon
         is never a drive letter.)
      3. If the split condition is True, perform import_path.rsplit(":", 1) to
         obtain (import_path, extension_name).
      4. Otherwise set extension_name = None and leave import_path unchanged.

      No other part of the function's behaviour (handling of built-in extensions,
      file-path loading via os.path.exists, dynamic import fallback, class/module
      scanning) may be altered.

requirements.description:
The `_load_extension` function currently handles colon-delimited extension paths
      (e.g., `path/to/file.py:ClassName`) but fails to correctly distinguish between:
      1. A Windows absolute path with a drive-letter colon (e.g., `C:/path/file.py`)
         that should NOT be split on the drive-letter colon when no class name is given.
      2. An absolute path with an explicit class name separator colon
         (e.g., `C:/path/file.py:ClassName` or `/abs/path/file.py:ClassName`)
         that SHOULD be split on the last colon to extract the class name.

      The current logic for detecting the colon separator does not use a
      platform-independent method to identify Windows drive letters, which leads to
      incorrect path splitting for absolute file paths. As a result, loading extensions
      from absolute paths (both with and without a `:ClassName` suffix) fails when
      the absolute path contains a colon (Windows drive letter).

      This is a correctness/parsing failure that prevents the following test cases
      from passing:
      - `Path("tests/test_extensions.py").absolute().as_posix()` (absolute path, no class name)
      - `Path("tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest").absolute().as_posix()` (absolute path with class name)
requirements.contract:
The `_load_extension` function must correctly parse the `import_path` string to
      distinguish between:

      A) A plain absolute or relative file path (no class name separator):
         - Examples: `/abs/path/to/ext.py`, `C:/path/to/ext.py`, `relative/ext.py`
         - Expected: `extension_name = None`; the file is loaded as a module and all
           Extension subclasses within it are discovered automatically.

      B) A file path with an explicit class name suffix separated by `:`:
         - Examples: `/abs/path/to/ext.py:MyExtension`, `C:/path/to/ext.py:MyExtension`,
           `relative/ext.py:MyExtension`
         - Expected: the path is split at the LAST colon to get the file path and the
           class name; only that named class is instantiated and returned.

      The disambiguation algorithm MUST use `pathlib.Path.drive` (or an equivalent
      platform-independent method) to detect Windows drive-letter colons:
      - A path with exactly one colon whose colon is part of the drive letter (e.g.,
        `Path(import_path).drive` contains `:`) must NOT be split; it is a plain
        Windows absolute path without a class name.
      - A path with more than one colon (Windows drive letter + class separator), or
        a path with one colon where that colon is NOT part of the drive letter (Linux/
        macOS absolute path with class separator), MUST be split using `rsplit(":", 1)`
        to extract the file path and the class name.

      The split must use `rsplit(":", 1)` (right-to-left, first occurrence from the
      right) rather than `split(":", 1)` to ensure that, on Windows, the drive-letter
      colon is preserved in the path portion (e.g., `"C:/path/file.py:ClassName"`
      splits into `["C:/path/file.py", "ClassName"]`).

      After the colon parsing, `os.path.exists(import_path)` is checked on the
      (possibly shortened) path to decide whether to load from file. This file-based
      loading must support both absolute and relative file paths.

      Function signature (unchanged): `_load_extension(extension) -> ExtensionType | list[ExtensionType]`

      Boundary conditions:
      - Zero colons: treat as plain path, `extension_name = None`.
      - One colon, drive letter contains `:` (Windows absolute path, no class): do not
        split, `extension_name = None`.
      - One colon, drive letter does not contain `:` (Linux/macOS with class name): split.
      - Two or more colons (Windows absolute path with class name): split using rsplit.

requirements.description:
The current colon-based parsing in `_load_extension` does not correctly distinguish
      between Windows drive-letter colons (e.g., `C:` in `C:/path/to/ext.py`) and the
      `filepath:ClassName` separator colon. A naive `":" in import_path` check will
      incorrectly split a Windows absolute path like `C:/path/to/ext.py` at the drive
      letter, producing `import_path = "C"` and `extension_name = "/path/to/ext.py"`,
      which causes the file-path lookup to fail. As a result, the parametrized test cases
      for absolute paths — both with and without an explicit class name suffix — are not
      resolved correctly and the tests fail.
requirements.contract:
The colon-splitting logic in `_load_extension` must be updated so that it
      correctly handles all of the following extension specification forms when the path
      portion is an absolute path (Windows or POSIX):

      1. `"/abs/path/to/ext.py"` — no colon separator; `extension_name` must be `None`.
      2. `"/abs/path/to/ext.py:ClassName"` — one colon separator (POSIX absolute path
         plus class name); must split into `import_path = "/abs/path/to/ext.py"` and
         `extension_name = "ClassName"`.
      3. `"C:/abs/path/to/ext.py"` — Windows drive letter; `extension_name` must be
         `None` (drive colon must NOT be treated as a separator).
      4. `"C:/abs/path/to/ext.py:ClassName"` — Windows absolute path plus class name;
         must split into `import_path = "C:/abs/path/to/ext.py"` and
         `extension_name = "ClassName"`.

      Behavioral contract:
      - Use `pathlib.Path(import_path).drive` to detect Windows drive letters
        platform-independently before deciding whether to split on a colon.
      - A colon should be treated as a `filepath:ClassName` separator only when:
        (a) there are more than one colon in the string, OR
        (b) there is exactly one colon AND that colon is NOT part of the detected
            drive (i.e., `":" not in Path(import_path).drive`).
      - After the split (or the decision not to split), all subsequent logic
        (builtin expansion, `os.path.exists` file-path loading, dotted-module import,
        extension class discovery) remains unchanged.
      - The function signature and return type are unchanged:
        `_load_extension(extension) -> ExtensionType | list[ExtensionType]`

requirements.description:
The _load_extension() function must correctly parse import_path strings that are
      absolute filesystem paths, both with and without a trailing ":ClassName" suffix.
      Currently the colon-detection logic uses Path(import_path).drive to distinguish
      Windows drive-letter colons from class-name separator colons, but the exact
      condition must cover all combinations:
        - 0 colons: treat as plain file path, no class name (Linux/Mac absolute path)
        - 1 colon AND the colon is part of a Windows drive letter: treat as plain file path
        - 1 colon AND the colon is NOT part of a Windows drive letter: split into file path
          + class name (e.g. /absolute/path/file.py:ExtensionTest)
        - 2 or more colons: always split on the last colon (Windows path with class name,
          e.g. C:/path/to/file.py:ExtensionTest)

      The split must use rsplit(":", 1) to extract at most one class-name suffix.
      After splitting, the file path portion must be checked for existence with os.path.exists()
      before falling back to dotted Python import path resolution.
requirements.contract:
Function signature: _load_extension(extension: str | dict | ExtensionType | type[ExtensionType]) -> ExtensionType | list[ExtensionType]

      Input cases for absolute path strings:
        - "/abs/path/to/module.py"
            → extension_name = None
            → os.path.exists("/abs/path/to/module.py") must be True for it to be loaded as a file
            → returns all Extension subclasses found in the module, each instantiated with no options
        - "/abs/path/to/module.py:SomeExtension"
            → import_path = "/abs/path/to/module.py", extension_name = "SomeExtension"
            → os.path.exists("/abs/path/to/module.py") must be True for it to be loaded as a file
            → returns getattr(loaded_module, "SomeExtension")() instantiated with no options
        - "C:/abs/path/to/module.py" (Windows)
            → extension_name = None (drive colon is NOT the class separator)
            → treated as plain file path
        - "C:/abs/path/to/module.py:SomeExtension" (Windows with class name)
            → colons == 2, so always split on last colon
            → import_path = "C:/abs/path/to/module.py", extension_name = "SomeExtension"
            → treated as file path + class name

      Colon detection contract:
        colons = import_path.count(":")
        split_on_colon = (colons > 1) or (colons == 1 and ":" not in Path(import_path).drive)
        If split_on_colon: import_path, extension_name = import_path.rsplit(":", 1)
        Else: extension_name = None

      If dict input: the single key is the import_path (possibly with :ClassName suffix),
      the value is a dict of keyword options passed to the extension constructor.

      The function must raise ExtensionNotLoadedError (not ImportError or AttributeError)
      when the file does not exist, the module cannot be imported, or the named class
      attribute does not exist in the loaded module.

requirements.description:
The `_load_extension` function must correctly handle absolute file paths passed as extension
      specifiers, both with and without a colon-separated class name suffix. Two sub-cases are
      currently non-passing:

      1. Absolute path WITH a class-name suffix — e.g. `/abs/path/ext.py:MyExtension`
         The colon-splitting logic must distinguish a colon that is a class-name separator from one
         that is part of a Windows drive letter (e.g. `C:` in `C:/path/file.py`). Failing to
         make this distinction causes the wrong `import_path` to be evaluated by `os.path.exists`,
         preventing the file from being found and the extension from loading.

      2. Absolute path WITHOUT a class-name suffix — e.g. `/abs/path/ext.py`
         When no colon is present the function must still recognise the string as a filesystem path
         via `os.path.exists` and load it accordingly, rather than falling through to the
         `dynamic_import` branch.

      The fix is particularly important on Windows (where drive letters introduce a colon) but the
      correct logic must also work on POSIX systems so that integration tests exercising absolute
      paths pass on all platforms.
requirements.contract:
`_load_extension(extension)` must satisfy the following behavioural contracts for file-path
      inputs:

      Colon detection and splitting:
      - POSIX absolute path with class name, e.g. `/abs/file.py:MyExt` (1 colon, no drive)
          → split: `import_path = "/abs/file.py"`, `extension_name = "MyExt"`
      - Windows absolute path with class name, e.g. `C:/abs/file.py:MyExt` (2 colons)
          → split at last colon: `import_path = "C:/abs/file.py"`, `extension_name = "MyExt"`
      - Windows absolute path without class name, e.g. `C:/abs/file.py` (1 colon, drive letter)
          → no split: `extension_name = None`
      - Relative or POSIX path without class name, e.g. `tests/ext.py` or `/abs/ext.py`
          → no split: `extension_name = None`

      The guard that prevents splitting on a Windows drive-letter colon must evaluate
      `Path(import_path).drive` on the full, pre-split `import_path`. If the single colon is
      part of the drive specification, the path must NOT be split.

      File loading (after colon processing):
      - If `os.path.exists(import_path)` is `True`, the module must be loaded via
        `_load_extension_path(import_path)`.
      - The `dynamic_import` fallback must only be reached when `os.path.exists` is `False`.

      Return values:
      - Absolute path + class name, file found:
          → Return a single instance: `getattr(loaded_module, extension_name)(**options)`
      - Absolute path, no class name, file found:
          → Search all attributes in the loaded module for subclasses of
            `(VisitorExtension, InspectorExtension, Extension)` that are not the base classes
            themselves, then return a list of instances (one per matching class).

code/src/griffe/extensions/base.py, function _load_extension — the section that

requirements.description:
The current colon-splitting logic in _load_extension uses a naive "if ':' in
      import_path" check to decide whether to split the path into a file path and a
      class name (e.g. "path/to/ext.py:MyExtension").  This fails for Windows absolute
      paths such as "C:/path/to/extension.py": the drive-letter colon ("C:") is
      mistakenly treated as the path/class-name separator, so rsplit(":", 1) produces
      import_path="C" and extension_name="/path/to/extension.py", causing the
      subsequent os.path.exists check to fail and the extension to not load.

      The same problem applies to Windows absolute paths that also carry a class name
      suffix: "C:/path/to/extension.py:MyClass" contains two colons (one for the drive
      letter and one for the class name), which must be handled separately.

      As a consequence, the two parametrised test cases that pass absolute file paths —
      both with and without an explicit class-name suffix — cannot succeed.
requirements.contract:
The _load_extension function must distinguish a Windows drive-letter colon from a
      path-to-class-name separator colon before attempting to split.

      Expected behaviour:

      1. Count the total number of colons in import_path.

      2. Use pathlib.PureWindowsPath(import_path).drive to obtain the drive component
         of the path (platform-independently).  A Windows drive letter contributes
         exactly one colon to the drive string (e.g. "C:").

      3. Apply the split only when:
           a. colons > 1  (handles "C:/path/to/ext.py:ClassName" — split on the last
              colon; the first belongs to the drive letter)
         OR
           b. colons == 1 AND ":" is NOT present in PureWindowsPath(import_path).drive
              (handles POSIX absolute paths like "/app/codes/ext.py:ClassName" where
              the single colon is truly a separator, not a drive letter)

      4. When neither condition holds (colons == 0 or the single colon belongs to the
         Windows drive), set extension_name = None and leave import_path unchanged.

      5. The import_path produced after any split must be the clean file-system path
         (e.g. "C:/path/to/extension.py" or "/app/codes/tests/ext.py"), suitable for
         os.path.exists.

      6. The extension_name produced after a split must be the bare class name string
         (e.g. "MyExtension"), suitable for getattr.

      Input/output contract summary:
        "C:/a/b/ext.py"          → import_path="C:/a/b/ext.py",  extension_name=None
        "C:/a/b/ext.py:MyClass"  → import_path="C:/a/b/ext.py",  extension_name="MyClass"
        "/abs/path/ext.py"       → import_path="/abs/path/ext.py", extension_name=None
        "/abs/path/ext.py:MyClass" → import_path="/abs/path/ext.py", extension_name="MyClass"
        "rel/path/ext.py:MyClass"  → import_path="rel/path/ext.py", extension_name="MyClass"

      No changes to any other part of _load_extension or to any other function are
      required for this fix.
